source: Learning Rsync - rsync demo 02: The rsync-protocol daemon and /etc/rsyncd.conf

questions.q: Why does rsync offer the rsync-protocol (virtual user) login instead of only SSH?
options: To avoid creating many system users, which is less secure -- it uses virtual users (like vsftpd)
questions.why: Many synchronization needs would mean many system users (more users = less secure); the rsync protocol uses virtual users instead.

questions.q: Which file do you create to configure the rsync daemon on Rocky Linux 8?
What does a [share] header define in rsyncd.conf?
options: A share (module) name that clients connect to
questions.why: [name] is the share/module name; clients target it by that name.

questions.q: What does `read only = yes` mean for a module (and how do you allow uploads)?
options: The share is read-only; set read only = no to allow read and write
It is read-write; yes allows uploads
questions.why: yes = read only, no = read and write.

options: Enables a virtual user named li for the share (you must create it)
questions.why: auth users names the virtual user(s); these are not system accounts.

questions.q: Where are the virtual users' passwords stored, and in what format?
options: In the secrets file (e.g. /etc/rsyncd_users.db), one 'username:password' per line, ending in .db
questions.why: The `secrets file` holds username:password lines and must end in .db.

questions.q: What permission must the rsync password (secrets) file have?
questions.why: The password file must be 600 (owner read/write only).

questions.q: Start the rsync daemon service (after installing rsync-daemon).
questions.why: systemctl start rsyncd.service starts the daemon; it then listens on port 873.
